title: Keeping turkeys on a smallholding
subtitle: What turkeys need, what they cost to keep, and where they differ from chickens.
excerpt: Turkeys are bigger, slower-growing, and more disease-prone than chickens, but they are good-tempered, grow fast relative to feed consumed, and produce birds that are worth considerably more per kilo than a standard meat hen. This reading covers the basics of housing, feeding, health risks, and slaughter logistics for a small home-raised flock.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Turkeys (2018). Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, turkey chapters. University of Wisconsin Extension, Raising Turkeys at Home.

glossaryTerms.definition: A young turkey, from hatching until it reaches approximately 8 weeks of age. Poults require heat, high-protein starter rations, and protection from wet conditions for the first few weeks.

Histomonosis — a parasitic disease caused by Histomonas meleagridis, transmitted via the caecal worm egg. Lethal to turkeys; chickens carry and shed the parasite with few symptoms. Keeping turkeys away from ground previously used by chickens is the primary prevention.

A male turkey. Tom is also used; stag is the UK smallholding term. A mature stag will be noticeably larger than the hens (jennies) and will display — fanning the tail and strutting — from about 12 weeks.

content.text: Turkeys produce a large table bird at a quality most commercial units cannot match. Management is broadly similar to chickens, but a few differences matter. Know them before the
poults
arrive and the raising period is far smoother.
Where turkeys differ from chickens
Bigger. An adult bronze or Norfolk Black turkey hen reaches 8-10 kg liveweight; a stag 12-18 kg. Feeders, drinkers, and housing all need to be turkey-sized, not repurposed chicken kit.

Slower to warm up. Poults are far less robust than chicks in their first two weeks. They need dry heat (around 35 degrees C at floor level in week one, dropping 3 degrees per week) and must not get wet.
Higher protein requirement. Poult starter ration needs to be 28% protein, which is higher than chick crumb. Using chick crumb slows growth significantly.
Susceptible to
blackhead disease
. Chickens carry and shed the Histomonas parasite without obvious illness; turkeys die from it. Never house turkeys on ground that chickens have grazed within two years.
Housing
A wooden shed with a solid roof and ventilation openings under the eaves works well. Allow at least 0.5 m per bird indoors; more if they are confined during wet weather.
Stags
will fight unless given adequate space, especially once dominance hierarchies form around 10 weeks. If you are keeping multiple stags, watch for injuries to wattles and legs.

Perches should be low for heavy breeds: 30-40 cm off the floor is enough. A heavy stag jumping down from a height can damage his feet or breast. Wide flat perches (8-10 cm flat face up) are kinder on the keel bone than round dowels.
Feeding

Weeks 0-8: 28% protein poult starter. Weeks 8-16: 20-22% protein grower pellet. Finishing weeks (typically weeks 16-24, depending on breed and target weight): 16-18% finisher. Ad-lib throughout. Turkeys raised entirely on fresh grass with supplementary grain rather than pellets will take longer to reach slaughter weight but are far less prone to overfat carcasses.
Slaughter and collection weights

For the Christmas table, poults sourced in late July or early August will be at target weight by mid-December. Traditional slow-growing breeds (Norfolk Black, Bronze, Narragansett) take longer than commercial breeds and reach a better carcase quality for the time. A oven-ready carcase weighs roughly 70-75% of the liveweight. Slaughter is via neck dislocation (for small birds up to around 5 kg liveweight) or by stun-kill, the same methods used for chickens. Farm-gate sale of home-raised poultry is permitted without abattoir involvement below 1,000 birds per year.
Health monitoring

Check the flock twice daily. Signs of blackhead disease include yellow, sulphur-coloured droppings and a bird standing apart from the flock with drooping head and wings. It progresses quickly; isolate any suspect bird and call a vet. Respiratory infections (Mycoplasma) are also more common in turkeys than chickens. Birds that show clear nasal discharge or laboured breathing should be separated promptly.
